query: does septoplasty affect middle turbinate hypertrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Rhinoplasty -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Turbinates
retrieved_docs.content: Within each nasal passage are superior, middle, and inferior turbinates, with the superior and middle being processes of the ethmoid bone and the inferior turbinate, or concha, being a separate bone altogether (see Image. Internal Nasal Anatomy). Most of the airflow passes below the middle and inferior turbinates. Conditions like rhinitis and septal deviation can induce turbinate hypertrophy, obstructing airflow. Turbinate reduction is commonly performed concurrently with septoplasty and septorhinoplasty.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Concha -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The mucosa also contains sensory nerves that allow the nose to feel airflow. When excessive damage to intranasal mucosal surfaces occurs, often due to overly aggressive turbinoplasty or turbinectomy, the nerves can no longer feel airflow. Affected individuals often complain of apparent nasal obstruction, even though the nasal passages may be widely patent. This condition is known as empty nose syndrome or atrophic rhinitis. Empty nose syndrome can significantly reduce a patient's quality of life and is sometimes treated with turbinate implantation to increase their bulk and resistance and the sensation of airflow within the nose. [22] [23] Thus, surgical reduction of the middle and inferior turbinates should be undertaken carefully, although the operation is often performed to alleviate nasal obstruction caused by inferior turbinate hypertrophy and middle turbinate concha bullosa.

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Acute Rhinosinusitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Septoplasty is a surgical option for patients with obstructive nasal septal deviations. The procedure reduces impingement of the sinus ostia, improves airflow, and facilitates proper mucociliary clearance. Turbinate reduction may alleviate nasal obstruction and allow further anterior drainage of nasal and sinus contents in patients with hypertrophy of the inferior turbinates.

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common long-term complaint after septoplasty with or without turbinoplasty is that the improvement in nasal breathing is insufficient; this may result from a number of reasons, including repeated trauma, weight gain, cartilage migration over time, and technical error, among others. [20] [21] Counseling patients about this risk pre-operatively is critical.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Concha -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of all or part of the inferior turbinate is common because of its role in nasal obstruction. Inferior turbinate hypertrophy in allergic rhinitis is often managed with intranasal steroid and antihistamine sprays. Surgical reduction of the inferior turbinates is necessary when medical management provides insufficient symptomatic relief, particularly in cases of compensatory inferior turbinate hypertrophy, which occurs in the setting of a deviated nasal septum. When the nasal septum deviates significantly to one side, the inferior turbinate on the concave side of the deviation often hypertrophies markedly to provide airflow regulation through that side of the nasal passage. In these cases, the soft tissue is engorged with blood, and the bone itself gains significant volume. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Dacryocystitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy is often an essential part of the clinical workup. The otorhinolaryngologist or a trained oculoplasty surgeon must examine for coexisting nasal pathologies such as nasal polyps, a deviated nasal septum, turbinate hypertrophy, atrophic rhinitis, signs of nasal trauma or past nasal surgeries, inflammatory sinus pathologies, fungal infections, or sinonasal malignancies. Endonasal examination also assesses the adequacy of the nasal cavity for future endonasal DCR surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Closure
retrieved_docs.content: Silastic splints are sometimes required. If necessary, these are cut to size, placed adjacent to the septum, and sutured transseptally for easy removal in the outpatient clinic. The splints aim to prevent adhesions to the turbinates if these are hypertrophied or if turbinoplasties have also been performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Obstruction -- History and Physical -- Anterior Rhinoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to assess for septal deviation, hypertrophy of the nasal turbinates, or nasal polyposis during anterior rhinoscopy. A quick test to prove choanal patency can be performed by asking the patient to exhale nasally onto a cold metal spatula (nasal misting test).

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In countries such as the UK, which operate on a state-funded healthcare system, it is imperative to demonstrate that such operations significantly improve patient quality of life and symptom burden to justify funding. Patients must be evaluated clinically, and the decision to proceed with surgery must be based upon evidence that suggests a good postoperative outcome is likely.

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of candidates for surgery is made on a clinical basis, and it is not entirely clear in the literature what prognostic factors determine successful postoperative outcomes. A combination of objective and subjective measures to identify appropriate patients may help improve outcomes in the future.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Disruption of the stability of the nasal valves from surgery or trauma may disrupt airflow dynamics or cause airway collapse or obstruction. A number of surgical techniques are available for nasal valve repair, including spreader grafts, butterfly grafts, and alar batten grafts. Hypertrophy of the anterior inferior turbinates is a common cause of nasal airway obstruction and can be present concurrently with nasal septal deviation and/or nasal valve collapse. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Septoplasty is one of the most frequently performed ENT procedures. Systematic reviews have revealed that long-term outcomes are favorable for most patients with septal deviation-related obstructive symptoms. Despite this, a significant proportion of patients experience recurrence of obstructive symptoms post-surgery, and satisfaction rates can be highly variable (50% to 100% satisfaction). [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Nasal Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: A three-pass technique is used to examine all areas of the nasal cavity. Septal deviation, bony spurs, turbinate hypertrophy External and internal nasal valve areas Nasal obstruction, mucopurulent debris, sinus drainage, mucosal edema Bleeding points, septal perforations Polyps, adhesions, and crusting

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Preparation -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be thoroughly examined in the outpatient clinic. A full head and neck examination should be carried out, followed by an anterior rhinoscopy with a nasal speculum. Flexible nasendoscopy can be performed to look for signs of sinonasal disease or masses in the posterior nasal space, particularly when the results of anterior rhinoscopy do not correspond to the patient history (e.g., severe nasal obstruction symptoms in the presence of normal-appearing turbinates and a midline anterior septum). When evaluating the nasal septum, the surgeon should look at the quality of the mucosa (assessing for signs of inflammation) and the size and nature of the turbinates, particularly the inferior turbinates. If there is poor access due to overly large turbinates, a turbinoplasty may also be indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhinitis Medicamentosa -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic ethmoiditis [15] Atrophic rhinitis [15] Septal perforation Chronic rhinosinusitis Turbinate hyperplasia

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are several contraindications to performing surgery. These include concurrent diseases such as rhinosinusitis or vasculitis or cases in which adequate medical therapy has not been attempted. While nasal sprays will not straighten a deviated septum, decreasing chronic inflammation may relieve symptomatic nasal obstruction sufficiently to obviate the need for operative intervention. Additionally, third-party payers are unlikely to approve an expensive surgical procedure if conservative therapy has not been exhausted first.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Beclomethasone, fluticasone and Momesalic are all members of the class of corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use. As intranasal glucocorticoids, they act on the nasal mucosa to suppress inflammatory mediators, reduce edema and relieve symptoms of conditions such as allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis and other inflammatory nasal disorders. Because they are applied locally rather than taken systemically, these agents provide effective anti‑inflammatory therapy with a lower risk of systemic side‑effects compared with oral corticosteroids, while sharing a common mechanism of glucocorticoid receptor activation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2725
retrieved_docs.content: Flutikason and ciclesonide are both classified as corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use. As members of this therapeutic class, they are intranasal glucocorticoids designed to deliver anti‑inflammatory effects directly to the nasal mucosa. These agents are commonly employed to manage conditions such as allergic rhinitis, nasal congestion, and nasal polyps by reducing mucosal edema, suppressing inflammatory mediator release, and improving nasal airflow. Their topical administration minimizes systemic exposure while providing targeted symptom relief, making them important options in the pharmacologic management of upper airway inflammatory disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر also has الرُعاف as a child concept, reinforcing the connection between unspecified nasal disorders and bleeding from the nose. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and functional network linking nasal‑septum disorders, their classification within upper‑respiratory disease taxonomy, and the corresponding diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those aimed at controlling epistaxis—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing nasal‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is classified as a type of “اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي” (nasal septum disorder). In the disease hierarchy, the label “other upper respiratory disease” is mapped to this same nasal septum disorder and is placed under the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders not classified elsewhere), which in turn is a parent of “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder). The nasal septum itself—referred to in Czech as “nosní přepážka” and abbreviated as “nos”—is identified as the finding site for “other upper respiratory disease” and serves as the parent concept for that disease group. Surgical interventions that manipulate a displaced nasal septum have a direct procedure site on the “nosní přepážka” (nasal septum). These interventions are organized as a child of the category “NOSE AND NASOPHARYNX: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES,” which is itself a child of the broader activity “control of epistaxis.” The same operative category includes the child “عمليات الحاجز الأنفي” (nasal septum surgeries). “Procedures on Nose” are linked to “اِضطِراب أنفي” (nasal disorder) and are considered a broader class that encompasses the specific activity of controlling epistaxis. The “control of epistaxis” procedure targets the nasal septum (“nos”) as its direct procedure site and is focused on treating “الرُعاف” (epistaxis), which is also listed as a child of the “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” category. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how anterior epistaxis, nasal septum pathology, and related surgical procedures are organized within a hierarchical framework of upper respiratory and nasal disorder classifications, emphasizing the central role of the nasal septum in both diagnosis and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1173
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with اِضطِراب أنفي, indicating that these interventions address nasal disorders. Within this broader category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of procedure (inverse_isa) that falls under Procedures on Nose. The control of epistaxis procedure is performed directly on the nos, meaning it targets the nose itself. Together, these relationships illustrate that managing nosebleeds is a direct, targeted intervention on the nose and forms part of the wider set of procedures used to treat nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2936
retrieved_docs.content: الرُعاف (epistaxis) is classified as a child entity within اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere), indicating that it is a specific type of nasal disorder that does not fit into other subcategories. Clinical attention to this condition centers on the control of epistaxis, which is identified as a primary focus of management for patients experiencing nasal bleeding. Recognizing الرُعاف as a distinct subset of unclassified nasal disorders facilitates targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at effectively controlling bleeding and improving patient outcomes.
